message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Importance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Nursing home (NH) residents are particularly vulnerable to SARS-CoV-2 infections and coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) lethality. However, excess deaths in this population have rarely been documented.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The primary objective was to assess the number of excess deaths among NH residents during the first wave of the COVID-19 pandemic in France. The secondary objectives were to determine the number of excess deaths as a proportion of the total excess deaths in the general population and determine whether a harvesting effect was present.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We studied a cohort of 494,753 adults (as of March 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    , 2020) aged 60 and over in 6,515 NHs in mainland France. This cohort was exposed to the first wave of the COVID-19 pandemic (from March 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    to May 31
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    , 2020) and was compared with the corresponding, reference cohorts from 2014 to 2019 (using data from the French National Health Data System).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main outcome and measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The main outcome was all-cause death. Weekly excess deaths and standardized mortality ratios (SMRs) were estimated.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Result</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    There were 13,505 excess deaths among NH residents. Mortality increased by 43% (SMR: 1.43). The mortality excess was higher among males than among females (SMR: 1.51 and 1.38, respectively) and decreased with age (SMRs in females: 1.61 in the 60-74 age group, 1.58 for 75-84, 1.41 for 85-94, and 1.31 for 95 or over; Males: SMRs: 1.59 for 60-74, 1.69 for 75-84, 1.47 for 85-94, and 1.41 for 95 or over). We did not observe a harvesting effect (up until August 30
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    , 2020). By extrapolating to all NH residents nationally (N=570,003), the latter accounted for 51% of the total excess deaths in the general population (N=15,114 out of 29,563).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>NH residents accounted for about half of the total excess deaths in France during the first wave of the COVID-19 pandemic. The excess death rate was higher among males than females and among younger residents than among older residents. We did not observe a harvesting effect. A real-time mortality surveillance system and the identification of individual and environmental risk factors might help to design the future model of care for older dependent adults.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Key points</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>During the first wave of the COVID-19 pandemic in France, the mortality among nursing home residents increased by 43%.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Nursing home residents accounted for 51% of the total excess deaths in France.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The excess mortality was higher among younger residents than among older residents.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The excess mortality was higher among males than among females.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>
                        We did not observe a harvesting effect during the study period (ending on August 30
                        <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                        , 2020, i.e., three months after the end of the first wave).
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Magnitude, change over time, demographic characteristics and geographic distribution of excess deaths among nursing home residents during the first wave of COVID-19 in France: a nationwide cohort study
